narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, family income level, and enrollment plans, while excluding gender and race from its calculations. The three biggest factors shaping this outcome were your Year 3 decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Most significantly, your Year 3 decile of 2 stood out as a strong push toward the failure prediction, since students who passed the bar typically ranked much higher, around the 6th decile on average—suggesting your later academic performance raised concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law school and the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though slightly below the 3.24 average of bar passers, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success, showing you have a solid foundation from your prior studies. Similarly, your LSAT score of 37, very close to the 37.27 average among those who passed, also helped pull the odds toward passing, highlighting your testing aptitude as a strength. While your high family income is less common among bar passers (only about 8.5% in that group) and your full-time enrollment aligns well with the 93% of passers, these played smaller roles compared to your grades and test scores. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by having competitive test results and background factors, but your lower Year 3 decile pulled you below the typical bar success benchmark, unlike most admits who maintained stronger decile progress throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar starting points improve dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted GPA-boosting programs to lift your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or community college refreshers that law schools value. Strengthening your academic trajectory now could shift future predictions in your favor, and reapplying after demonstrable gains in later performance has succeeded for others in your position. You're clearly driven, with a high family support structure that positions you well—focus on that Year 3 momentum, and you'll have a compelling story for the next round.
